data.title: Newsom says world sees US as 'a wrecking ball' under Trump
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) suggested Friday that world leaders likely see the U.S. as a “wrecking ball” under its current leadership, but added that President Trump is “temporary.”

Newsom, on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference, said the people he spoke with during the summit signaled they no longer see the U.S. as the leader of the free world.

“They see us as a wrecking ball,” he told CNN’s Kasie Hunt. “They see us as unreliable, and a lot of them think irrevocable. They don’t think we’ll ever come back to our original form.”

“I’m not as convinced of that. Whatever happens, we can undo, we can shapeshift, we can fix it,” Newsom added.

The Democratic governor said the U.S.-Europe relationship will not “be undone permanently,” noting that Trump will be out of office in three years.

“Well, I’m here in many respects to remind everyone that Trump is temporary,” he said. “He’ll be gone in a matter of years. States like California are permanent. We’re reliable, stable partners.”

Newsom also dismissed Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s remarks at the conference when he said the “old world is gone.”

“I think it’s not dead, it’s dormant,” the governor told Hunt. “It’s waiting for different conditions to move forward, and I think that changes in the midterms, I think tonally begins to shift a little bit after November.”

“Trump’s going to get shellacked in the midterms. He knows that,” he continued. “The world, I think, is becoming more and more familiar with that reality.”

Newsom also called Trump an “increasingly weak” leader who is “masquerading as strong,” which he thinks “will open the door again to diplomacy and common sense.”

The California Democrat is one of multiple potential 2028 presidential contenders, including Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) — who also spoke in Munich — and Sen. Mark Kelly (D-Ariz.).

Newsom did not close the door on a possible run, but when asked about his plans said “I honestly don’t know.” He added that he’s “trying to be accountable to the world I’m living in.”

“But look, this moment requires, I think, leadership, it requires a counternarrative,” the governor said.

“Trump does not represent the majority of Americans,” he continued. “The most historic president in our lifetime [is] historically unpopular, and the rest of the world needs to understand that.”

Newsom already raised his opposition to Trump to the world stage last month when he attended the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. The governor slammed Trump’s speech about the U.S. acquiring Greenland, calling it “remarkably boring,” but also called the European response to Trump “pathetic,” saying it’s “time to buck up.”

“It’s time to get serious,” the Democrat said. “And stop being complicit. It’s time to stand tall and firm, have a backbone. You know, I’ve seen this in the United States, supine Congress, playing both sides. You know, say one thing on a text or a tweet, another publicly.”

The governor said he received praise the next day from world leaders.

Trump clapped back at Newsom over his appearance at Davos, accusing the California leader of “running around screaming for the attention of Foreign Leaders, and embarrassing our Country.”

data.title: In Munich, Democrats pledge a return to wealth taxes and climate hysteria
data.description:
This week, California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) joined the many Californians now seeking their fortune elsewhere. The difference is that Newsom is planning to come back to California, even as billionaires, investors, and companies flee his state for greener pastures.

Newsom and Democrats such as Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) were selling a brave new world that looked a lot like the broken old world. It was an ironic moment. They were addressing countries at the Munich Security Conference that had previously destroyed their economies through socialist and far-left policies. 

The rush of liberal Democratic officeholders to Europe was telling. A new poll shows that a record 58 percent of voters believe their party is “too liberal.” But Newsom and Ocasio-Cortez found a welcoming audience in Europe. 

The global elite gushed over Ocasio-Cortez and sat enraptured as she rattled off socialist platitudes. That included New York Times correspondent Katrin Bennhold, who thrilled the audience by treating it as a given that Ocasio-Cortez will run for president. 

Both Newsom and Ocasio-Cortez spoke of returning the U.S. to the good graces of the global elite. Newsom assured the Europeans that Trump’s reign is temporary, and that the U.S. will soon enough dismantle the “wrecking ball” that the administration has taken to the EU. 

Newsom offered his leadership and his state as the model, proclaiming that “California is a stable and reliable partner” for Europe. The model includes high taxes, massive spending programs and greater bureaucratic regulations — precisely the policies that have driven the European economy into its current stagnation. In other words, Democrats were in Europe to offer precisely what Newsom outwardly condemned: “doubling down on stupid.”

When not fumbling with security questions about issues such as Taiwan, Ocasio-Cortez was demanding that wealth taxes be implemented in the U.S. “expeditiously.” Such a tax on billionaires’ wealth, including unrealized gains, is currently being pushed in California. The predictable result is that billionaires and other wealthy citizens are rushing to leave the state and taking their investments and companies with them.

Ocasio-Cortez had the audience at hello.

Rather than having Vice President J.D. Vance shaming them for their attacks on free speech, the Europeans positively gushed over Democratic leaders pushing far-left agendas. It did not matter that such policies devastated European economies in the 20th century.

In my book “Rage and the Republic,” I discuss the rise of support for socialism in both the U.S. and Europe. Many of those supporting it are young voters with no memory of the collapse of socialist economies in the 20th Century. In 1977, Labour Prime Minister James Callaghan pursued many of the same socialist policies, leading to what was called the “winter of discontent” as inflation hit 25 percent. With the collapse of the British pound, the United Kingdom had to take the demoralizing step of securing a loan from the International Monetary Fund, as if it were a developing country.

In France, François Mitterrand was also elected to pursue his “rupture with capitalism.” The French economy collapsed; Mitterrand quickly had to reverse himself and restore capitalist policies.

That history is rarely discussed or taught today. The “warmth of collectivism,” as New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani put it, is back in vogue. It does not matter that, in Argentina, President Javier Milei is achieving one of the most impressive economic turnarounds in history — dramatically curtailing runaway inflation, government deficits and poverty — by reinstating free-market policies and reducing government spending.

What is chilling about Europe is that the EU has strangled growth with its increasingly centralized controls and massive bureaucracy. My book describes the instability of the EU and its global governance model. Europe is facing populist movements and, like many Democrats, the response has been calls for further consolidation of power. This included the creation of a new, uniform European corporate law, known as the “28th Regime.”

With an economy crushed by a massive EU bureaucracy and regulations, the solution of many is all too familiar: borrow more money. French President Emmanuel Macron and others want to issue “Euro bonds” to spend their way into an economic recovery — another policy ideal shared with many on the American left. 

This week was only the latest effort of the American left to strengthen an alliance with the EU. Previously, American leaders such as Hillary Clinton pushed the EU to censor Americans online after free speech protections were restored by companies like Twitter. Likewise, the American left is enamored with the EU’s global bureaucracy and regulations.

Newsom and Ocasio-Cortez certainly found their element in Munich, and the EU certainly found the “reliable partners” it has longed for in creating “a new World Order with European Values.”

Jonathan Turley is the Shapiro Professor of Public Interest Law at George Washington University. He is the author of the New York Times bestselling “Rage and the Republic: The Unfinished Story of the American Revolution.”

data.title: Rubio seeks unity with Europe, blasts 'dangerous delusions' on migration, climate
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio on Saturday sought to reassure Europe that the U.S. still wanted to work together but warned allies against “dangerous delusions” on migration and climate change.

Rubio, during his speech at the annual Munich Security Conference, said the U.S. is “a child of Europe” and they “belong together.”

“We believe that Europe must survive, because the two great wars of the last century serve for us as history’s constant reminder that ultimately, our destiny is and will always be intertwined with yours,” he said, Bloomberg reported.

Rubio added that the “euphoria” at the end of the Cold War resulted in a “dangerous delusion that we had entered ‘the end of history'” where nations would “live in a world without borders where everyone became a citizen of the world.”

The secretary of state also noted that President Trump “demands seriousness and reciprocity from our friends here in Europe,” with “no interest in being polite and orderly caretakers of the west’s managed decline.”

“We do not seek to separate, but to revitalize an old friendship and renew the greatest civilization in human history,” Rubio said, The Guardian reported. “What we want is a reinvigorated alliance that recognizes that what has ailed our societies is not just a set of bad policies, but a malaise of hopelessness and complacency.”

Rubio also slammed the “climate cult” as a “foolish idea that ignored both human nature and it ignored the lessons of over 5,000 years of recorded human history, and it has cost us dearly.”

The top U.S. diplomat’s tone differed from Vice President Vance’s speech to allies during the same conference last year. Vance accused Europe of backing off from “fundamental values” and using laws to disenfranchise populist voters and voices over misinformation and disinformation.

“I think it’s the same message,” Rubio told Bloomberg, when asked about his speech compared to Vance’s. “I think what the Vice President said last year very clearly was that Europe had made a series of decisions internally that were threatening to the alliance and ultimately to themselves, not because we hate Europe or we don’t like Europeans but because — what is it that we fight for, what is it that binds us together?”

He added, “And ultimately, it’s the fact that we are both heirs to the same civilization.”

The speech comes amid strained relations between the U.S. and European allies after Trump’s return to office last year. Threats to take over Greenland and, what German Chancellor Friedrich Merz on Friday called “lost” leadership from the U.S., has strained relations.

Merz appealed to Trump that repairing ties with Europe is the strongest defense against an advancing Chinese military.

“In the era of great power rivalry, even the United States will not be powerful enough to go it alone,” he said during his speech in Munich.

The chancellor also called for a “new transatlantic partnership” among European countries while also appealing for repairing relations with the U.S. to keep NATO strong. He referred to Vance’s 2025 speech, saying he was right about the “divide” between the U.S. and Europe.

He then pushed back on the vice president’s remarks on misinformation and disinformation.

“The battle of cultures, of MAGA in the U.S., is not ours,” Merz said. “Freedom of speech here ends where the words spoken are directed against human dignity and our basic law.”

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: MAP: Here's how much babysitters make in 2026. Are you overpaying?
data.description:
How much should you tip on a takeout order?

(NEXSTAR) – Along with finding someone you can trust, hiring a babysitter with an affordable rate is also crucial for parents – after all, it could determine whether you can afford to go out in the first place.

Where you live can influence how much – or little – you can expect to pay, according to a study by UrbanSitter, a platform that connects parents with caregivers.

If you’re wondering where your favorite sitter’s rate stacks up against the national average, UrbanSitter internal data shows the hourly rate to be $26.24 for one child, and $29.87 for two children.

If that sounds high, it may be because the national average jumped 4.9% over the last year, outpacing inflation which rose nearly 3%, according to the report.

The three priciest cities (for one child) were San Francisco ($29.63/hr), Seattle ($27.70/hr) and Honolulu ($27.22/hr), while the three most affordable were San Antonio ($18.21/hr), Dallas/Ft. Worth ($19.25/hr), Columbus ($19.98/hr).

What about tipping?

Beyond the hourly rate, there is always the question of tipping.

“In my years of babysitting for regular families and working as an on-call sitter for private homes, special events, and hotels, I would not say that it’s customary to receive a tip from regular families each time they are babysitting,” Erin Carroll-Manning, founder of Boston-based Gentle Giraffes Newborn Care, told Care.com.

While one-off evenings out may not require gratuity, UrbanSitter told Nexstar in an email that when tips are given, the average amount is 20%.

Experts say there are also special occasions during the year when it’s appropriate to give your child’s regular caretaker a tip.

Carroll-Manning added that a holiday bonus “is always appreciated” and the Emily Post Institute advises tipping an au pair or daily babysitter “A gift from your family (or one week’s pay) plus a small gift from your child.”

The bonus can be given at the end of summer or during the holidays, but needn’t be given at both, etiquette experts say.

data.title: Don't Let Jimmy Lai become another Liu Xiaobo
data.description:
Jimmy Lai has been sentenced to 20 years in prison. His crimes, according to Hong Kong authorities, were “collusion with foreign forces” and “hostile activities.” But who are these “foreign hostile forces”? In Beijing’s narrative, they are primarily the U.S. — and, by extension, the democratic world.

That uncomfortable truth carries a deeper implication: If Washington is cast as the alleged co-conspirator in Lai’s “crime,” then the United States cannot simply stand aside as a distant observer. America is, in the eyes of the Chinese state, part of the story. That means it also bears responsibility for how the story ends. 

The lesson of recent history is sobering. In 2017, during Donald Trump’s first presidential term, Nobel Peace Prize laureate Liu Xiaobo died in Chinese custody from liver cancer. Despite appeals from governments and human rights advocates around the world, the Chinese authorities refused to let him travel abroad for treatment. The free world failed to secure even that minimal humanitarian concession. 

Liu’s death was more than a personal tragedy. It marked a moral defeat for the democratic world — a moment when the most basic principle of human dignity was subordinated to geopolitical calculation and diplomatic caution. 

Now, nearly a decade later, the U.S. faces another such test. 

Jimmy Lai is 78 years old. He has spent years in detention under harsh conditions. If he dies in prison, it will not simply be another entry in a human rights report; it will be a symbolic moment, a signal that even the world’s leading democracy cannot protect those whom it openly calls friends and allies. 

But this time, circumstances are different. And the United States has more leverage than it did in 2017. 

Both Trump and Xi Jinping are transactional leaders. Each has shown a preference for large, dramatic deals over slow, incremental diplomacy. Both measure success in tangible outcomes: trade balances, strategic concessions, and visible political wins. 

Trump’s reported April visit to China offers an opening. 

The geopolitical environment is shifting in ways that could increase Beijing’s incentives to reach an understanding with Washington. Japan’s recent election has strengthened political forces advocating constitutional revision, including potential changes to Article Nine — the “peace clause” that has defined Japan’s postwar military posture. If Japan moves toward becoming a more conventional military power, tensions between Beijing and Tokyo are likely to intensify. 

Chinese strategic culture has long emphasized the principle of “befriend the distant, strike the near.” If tensions with Japan increase, Beijing may seek to stabilize relations with the U.S. to avoid facing pressure from two powerful rivals at once.

In such a context, a humanitarian gesture — such as releasing Jimmy Lai — could become a low-cost concession for Beijing, especially if it is folded into a broader diplomatic or economic package. 

The key is to approach the issue not as a moral lecture but as a strategic opportunity. 

Washington should quietly make Lai’s release a concrete objective of any high-level engagement with Beijing. It should not be framed as an ultimatum or a condition for talks, but as a deliverable — something both sides can present as a positive outcome. 

For Trump, the benefits are obvious. Securing Lai’s release would be a dramatic, headline-grabbing success. It would distinguish his second presidency from his first, when Liu Xiaobo died in custody. It would also provide a powerful answer to critics who argue that his foreign policy neglects human rights. 

More importantly, it would demonstrate that the United States still has the ability, and the will, to protect those who stand with it. 

For Beijing, the costs are relatively small. Jimmy Lai is already an elderly man whose media empire has been dismantled and whose political influence has been neutralized. Releasing him on medical parole or allowing him to leave Hong Kong would not change the balance of power in the territory. But it could yield significant diplomatic goodwill at a moment when China may want to ease tensions with Washington. 

In other words, this is a deal that both sides could live with. 

If Lai dies in prison, the consequences will be far more damaging. It would reinforce the perception, in Hong Kong, Taiwan and beyond, that American promises of support carry little practical weight — proof that even those accused of “colluding” with the United States, like Lai, are ultimately disposable in the eyes of the very country named as their alleged partner. It would deepen cynicism among democratic activists worldwide, and hand Beijing a propaganda victory.

For Trump personally, the stakes are just as high. History will inevitably compare the fate of Jimmy Lai with that of Liu Xiaobo. If another prominent pro-democracy figure dies in Chinese custody during his presidency, it will overshadow any trade deal or diplomatic achievement. 

But if Lai walks free, the narrative changes. 

It becomes a story of leverage used wisely, of a life saved, and of American power exercised for a moral purpose without sacrificing strategic interests. 

In diplomacy, the most meaningful victories are often the simplest ones: a prisoner released, a life spared, a symbol preserved. 

Jimmy Lai’s fate is not sealed. But time is running out. 

President Trump has an opportunity—perhaps the last one—to ensure that this story ends differently. 

Jianli Yang is a research fellow at Harvard Kennedy School of Government and a columnist for National Review. In December 2010, Jianli Yang represented the imprisoned Liu Xiaobo at his Nobel Peace Prize ceremony in Oslo, Norway.

data.title: Latest US strike on alleged drug vessel kills 3 'narco-terrorists'
data.description:
The U.S. military on Friday said it carried out a strike on an alleged drug-smuggling vessel in the Caribbean, killing three — months after similar strikes caused controversy at the Defense Department.

The vessel was operated by designated terrorist organization and moved along “known narco-trafficking routs in the Caribbean and was engaged in narco-trafficking operations,” U.S. Southern Command (Southcom) said in a statement shared on social platform X.

No U.S. military personnel were injured in the incident.

Southcom commander Gen. Francis L. Donovan directed the strike as part of “Operation Southern Spear.” The X post included video of the boat traveling before it was struck and then exploded.

It is unclear which terrorist group Southcom referred to.

The fatalities from the latest strike raises the death toll from the Trump administration’s strikes on alleged drug boats to 133 people since September. Friday’s attack was the second this week after Southcom conducted a strike in the Eastern Pacific that killed two “narco-terrorists” and left one survivor.

Last week, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth wrote online that “some top cartel drug-traffickers” chose to conclude “all narcotics operations INDEFINITELY due to recent (highly effective) kinetic strikes in the Caribbean.” The text is preceded by “WINNING.”

Hegseth and the Pentagon came under fire last year after an initial strike on an alleged drug boat in September damaged a vessel and left two survivors before a second strike was ordered that killed them both.

The Trump administration said Adm. Frank Bradley authorized the second strike, with Hegseth insisting he was not in the room when the second attack happened. The Washington Post reported that Hegseth authorized the second strike with the directive to “kill everybody,” which Bradley denied before lawmakers during a briefing on Capitol Hill in December.

“Admiral Bradley was very clear that he was given no such order, not to give no quarter or to kill them all,” Sen. Tom Cotton (R-Ark.), the chair of the Senate Select Committee on Intelligence, said after the classified briefing with Bradley. “He was given an order that, of course, was written down in great detail, as our military always does.”

Rep. Jim Himes (D-Conn.), the top Democrat on the House Intelligence Committee, also said Bradley confirmed he was not given any order to conduct the second strike. He said, however, that he was “deeply troubled” after reviewing video of the attack.

“The fact is that we killed two people who were in deep distress and had neither the means nor obviously the intent to continue their mission,” Himes said.

Democrats on the House and Senate Armed Services committees launched a review of the attack, arguing that the second strike amounted to a war crime.

House Democrats introduced two bills in December to curtail the strikes and the administration’s “hostilities within or against Venezuela,” prior to the U.S. strikes on the country’s capital Caracas and the capture of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro on Jan. 3.

Both resolutions, introduced by House Foreign Affairs Committee ranking member Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.) and House Rules Committee ranking member Jim McGovern (D-Mass.), respectively, failed to pass.

data.title: Katie Holmes, 'Dawson's Creek' crew pay tribute to James Van Der Beek: 'A Hero'
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Katie Holmes called her late “Dawson’s Creek” co-star James Van Der Beek a “hero” in an emotional open letter posted to Instagram.

Van Der Beek, 48, died from complications from colorectal cancer on Wednesday.

Holmes, that night, posted a picture of a handwritten letter addressed to “James,” saying she wrote the message with a “heavy heart.”

“Thank you,” she began the letter. “To share space with your imagination is sacred — breathing the same air in the land of make believe and trusting that each others’ hearts are safe in their expression.”

James Van Der Beek and Katie Holmes are pictured at the GLAAD Media Awards in 2008. (SGranitz/WireImage)

Holmes alluded to “laughter,” “conversations” and “James Taylor songs” the two had bonded over in their “unique youth.”

She then called Van Der Been a “hero.”

“Bravery. Compassion, Selflessness. Strength. An appreciation for life and the action taken to live life with the integrity that life is art — creating a beautiful marriage, six loving children  — the journey of a Hero,” the actress wrote.

“I mourn this loss with a heart holding the reality of his absence and deep gratitude for his imprint on it,” she added, before addressing Van Der Beek’s widow Kimberly. “To Kimberly and the children, we are here for you always. And will always he there to shower you with Love and Compassion.”

Holmes and Van Der Beek had starred together on “Dawson’s Creek” for all six seasons of the series.

‘Dawson’s Creek’ family remembers James Van Der Beek

The show’s creator Kevin Williamson also paid tribute to Van Der Beek, saying it was difficult for him to put his feelings into words.

“Several times today, from my heart, I’ve tried to form the words to express the beautiful brilliance of James and what his presence has meant to my life. But I am truly at a loss for words,” he wrote on Instagram next to an image of him and Van Der Beek. “I will have to trust that one day those words will come.”

Actress Busy Philipps, who also appeared on “Dawson’s Creek,” shared her own message on Instagram.

“James Van Der Beek was one in a billion and he will be forever missed and I don’t know what else to say. I am just so so sad,” she wrote. “He was my friend and I loved him and I’m so grateful for our friendship all these years.”

The late actor, who shares six kids with his wife, revealed his cancer diagnosis to People in November 2024, which he discovered during a routine colonoscopy.

“I’ve been privately dealing with this diagnosis and have been taking steps to resolve it, with the support of my incredible family,” he told the outlet at the time.

A GoFundMe page that was started following his death has now raised over $2.5 million for his family.

data.title: Abandoning negotiations won't stop Trump's attacks on clean energy
data.description:
During January’s Winter Storm Fern, power outages surged across the eastern United States “amid restricted gas supplies” and a hydropower transmission line known as New England Clean Energy Connect shut down. 

Some will likely use this example to attack renewables, but America needs all the electrons it can muster. At a time of emergency for our grid, Winter Storm Fern should serve as an important reminder that it’s simply irresponsible not to build the infrastructure America needs to deliver this essential energy and keep our communities safe.

That’s why Sens. Martin Heinrich (D-N.M.) and Sheldon Whitehouse (D-R.I.) are right to call out the Trump administration’s reckless assault on renewable energy. The suspension of offshore wind projects and the stalling of over 500 clean energy projects is reckless and will hurt American workers and communities that desperately need affordable power.

While I understand the temptation to disengage from Trump’s maddening behavior, I respectfully disagree with their decision to walk away from the permitting reform negotiating table. If Democrats aren’t at the table, we’ll be on the menu. That means Trump will have free rein to weaponize the permitting process while blaming Democrats for the chaos. We can’t let that happen.

In a recent speech on the Senate floor, Heinrich shared a powerful story from New Mexico that illustrates exactly why permitting reform is so urgent and necessary for this challenge. As he explained, workers are completing a massive 3.5-gigawatt wind farm and 550-mile transmission line — a project equivalent to three and a half nuclear reactors and bigger than the Hoover Dam. Yet it took an astonishing and unacceptable 17 years to get the permits needed to build it. That is insanity.

And this isn’t just about one project in New Mexico. Democrats invested billions through the Inflation Reduction Act to jumpstart America’s clean energy future. But right now, those investments are stuck in permitting purgatory. Solar farms, wind installations and transmission lines that could power our communities and create middle-class jobs are languishing for years. If we walk away from permitting reform, we’re essentially abandoning the very projects we fought so hard to fund.

This approach also supports environmental justice. A recent Progressive Policy Institute report makes clear that low-income Black and Latino families are forced to spend higher shares of their smaller incomes on energy compared to wealthier neighborhoods, and face energy insecurity at double the level of white households. If we want to continue to be the party that fights for the marginalized and economic disadvantaged, we must get permitting reform done.

We need to call Trump’s bluff and stay at the table. That’s the only way to negotiate protections that prevent any administration from weaponizing the permitting process and fight for provisions that accelerate clean energy while maintaining essential environmental safeguards. And if Republicans refuse those terms, we should make them own that refusal and suffer the political consequences when energy costs continue to spike.  

Democrats must be the party that changes the game on energy and delivers affordable energy, good jobs and climate solutions. We just can’t do that from the sidelines.

Heinrich and Whitehouse have been tireless advocates for clean energy and bipartisan permitting reform. They are some of the most intelligent and thoughtful members of the Senate.

I hope they will reconsider their position and return to negotiations. Because if we’re not at the table shaping permitting reform, Trump and his allies will shape it for us — and working families will pay the price.

Tim Ryan served as a Democratic U.S. Representative from Ohio’s 13th Congressional District from 2003 to 2023.

data.title: How authorities got Nancy Guthrie camera footage through 'backend systems'
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Days after authorities said the footage could not be retrieved, the FBI released surveillance video of a masked, armed person approaching Nancy Guthrie’s home, saying it was recovered from “backend systems.” 

Pima County Sheriff Chris Nanos initially said that a Google Nest camera attached to Guthrie’s door was disconnected just before 2 a.m. on the night she disappeared. While the camera’s software detected movement minutes later, no footage was preserved, he said.

At the time, Nanos noted there was no video available in part because Guthrie, the mother of NBC host Savannah Guthrie, didn’t have an active subscription to the company. 

But then FBI Director Kash Patel said Tuesday that investigators kept working for days to pull the videos from “residual data located in backend systems.”

Adam Wandt, associate professor and deputy chair for technology at John Jay College of Criminal Justice in New York City, explained that the footage was retrieved through forensic analysis of data stored on the servers.

The search for Guthrie is in its second week. Authorities say the 84-year-old was likely kidnapped or abducted from her home the night before she was reported missing on Jan. 31. 

This week, the FBI released photos of a masked person seen in the camera footage, described as a subject in the investigation.

Where did the footage go on a non-subscription security camera?

Without a premium subscription, Nest cameras don’t save video history, though live views and alerts still work, Wandt said. 

NOW PLAYING

When motion is detected, the feed is streamed to Google for processing. For premium users, the video is saved, but for non-premium users, it’s marked for deletion, he noted. This is usually the case for other security cameras, including Ring, he said.

Because all footage is processed in the cloud, it temporarily exists on Google’s servers. Everything up until that connection was lost from the camera was already processed. 

“What Google was able to do in this case is do forensics on the servers that process this data in order to find videos that were never saved but processed by the system,” Wandt added.

The video from the Google Nest camera showed an individual wearing a full face mask, gloves and a backpack walking up to the home with what appears to be a flashlight in their mouth and a gun in a front holster.

The person is seen placing a gloved hand over the camera lens, then using a nearby shrub to further conceal the device.

Why was the footage initially called ‘inaccessible’?

Wandt noted that in criminal cases where camera evidence is needed, there are often multiple rounds of legal back-and-forth between investigators and a company.

“Without that subscription, they got a response that it’s inaccessible; at that point, it’s up to either the government or Google to say, but we might be able to have this extra stuff,” Wandt said. 

“It makes sense that there were multiple rounds of back and forth where they were first told, ‘no, we cannot get you this file,’ and then, through refining the process, came up with something where maybe they could retrieve something.”

That takes time, which explains how the footage turned up days after, he said. 

Google’s recovery of the footage went beyond normal operations: Expert

Google wasn’t intentionally trying to save this footage, but it was there before processing, and so they were able to go in with forensics and pull it, Wandt said.

Getting a forensic team to dig this footage out of the server was outside of Google’s normal systems and workflows, he noted.

NOW PLAYING

“I think it’s clear that Americans’ data is not at risk, that Google is not saving a bunch of things that they’re not supposed to save,” he added. “Google went above and beyond and retrieved and recovered something that they normally would not have been able to do because of the high profile of this case.”

data.title: The 10 college majors with the lowest unemployment
data.description:
(NewsNation) — The college majors with the lowest unemployment rates tend to share one thing in common: They rely on face-to-face work.

The findings come from a new analysis by the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, which examined outcomes for recent college graduates across 73 majors using 2024 Census data.

Four of the 10 majors with the lowest unemployment were in education, led by special education at 0.7 percent — the lowest rate among all fields measured.

Other people-facing majors, including social services (1.9 percent) and nursing (2.1 percent), also posted unemployment well below the 4.2% overall rate for recent college grads.

While the analysis reflects 2024 conditions, it aligns with recent hiring trends that continue to favor roles built around direct interaction. The health care and social assistance sector has accounted for nearly all of the private-sector job growth over the past year.

Part of that growth is driven by the nation’s rapidly aging population, which is expected to make nurses, physical therapist assistants and physician assistants among the fastest growing roles over the next decade, according to Labor Department projections.

Still, the New York Fed’s study suggests there’s a trade-off between job security and pay: 8 of the 10 lowest-unemployment majors earned below $58,000, the median early-career wage for recent college graduates in 2024.

Special education majors with only a bachelor’s degree earned about $46,000 early in their careers, while social services majors brought in $43,000.

Nursing ($70,000) and engineering technologies ($65,000) were exceptions, combining low unemployment with above-average pay.

The New York Fed also tracked “underemployment,” or the share of graduates working in jobs that typically don’t require a college degree. Nursing stood out here as well, with the lowest underemployment rate of any major at just 12.8 percent.

Various engineering majors also had low underemployment rates, below 20 percent, indicating strong alignment between degrees and jobs.

The New York Fed defined recent college grads as those aged 22 to 27 with a bachelor’s degree or higher.

Learn about the college majors with the highest unemployment rates here.

Lowest unemployment rates for recent college grads by major in 2024 (NY Fed analysis of Census Bureau data)

  • Overall: 4.2 percent
  1. Special Education: 0.7 percent
  2. Miscellaneous Education: 1.1 percent
  3. Elementary Education: 1.2 percent
  4. Agriculture: 1.4 percent
  5. Foreign Language: 1.6 percent
  6. Geography: 1.6 percent
  7. Engineering Technologies: 1.7 percent
  8. Social Services: 1.9 percent
  9. Nursing: 2.1 percent
  10. Secondary Education: 2.1 percent

Lowest underemployment rates for recent college grads by major in 2024 (NY Fed analysis of Census Bureau data)

  • Overall: 39.4 percent
  1. Nursing: 12.8 percent
  2. Aerospace Engineering: 14.7 percent
  3. Civil Engineering: 15.6 percent
  4. Computer Engineering: 15.8 percent
  5. Special Education: 16.0 percent
  6. Elementary Education: 16.2 percent
  7. Chemical Engineering: 17.9 percent
  8. Construction Services: 17.9 percent
  9. Computer Science: 19.1 percent
  10. Mechanical Engineering: 20.1 percent
